metadata.description: Essential Chinese vocabulary for appointments, phone calls, and communication methods

content.name: to_for
content.side_f: I'll call you. (lit. I give you hit telephone.)
content.side_g: 给 is a versatile preposition meaning 'to/for' introducing a recipient; it also functions as a verb meaning 'to give'. In phone/message contexts 给 + person + 打电话/发短信 is the standard pattern. Distinct from 对 (duì, 'toward') which is used for speech directed at someone but not for benefactive actions.

content.name: make_phone_call
content.side_e: Nǐ wǎnshang gěi wǒ dǎ diànhuà, hǎo ma?
content.side_f: Call me tonight, OK? (lit. hit telephone)
content.side_g: 打电话 is a verb-object compound; the object 电话 can be split from 打 by duration/aspect markers: 打了一个电话 ('made a phone call'). Use 给 + person before 打电话 to mark the recipient. Colloquial variants include 来电话 (incoming) and 回电话 (call back).

content.name: hello_phone
content.side_e: Wéi, nín hǎo, qǐng wèn nín zhǎo nǎ wèi?
content.side_f: Hello, who are you looking for? (lit. Hello, you good, please ask you seek which [polite-MW]?)
content.side_g: 喂 is used almost exclusively to open phone calls or to get someone's attention. Second tone (wéi) is the polite/standard telephone greeting; fourth tone (wèi) is more abrupt, like 'Hey!' It is not used face-to-face as a greeting — use 你好 instead.

content.name: be_present
content.side_a: to be present; to be at (a place)
content.side_e: Lǎoshī zài ma? — Tā bú zài.
content.side_f: Is the teacher in? — She's not in.
content.side_g: 在 has three uses: (1) main verb 'to be at/present' (他在家); (2) preposition before a location (在学校学习); (3) progressive-aspect marker 'be -ing' (他在吃饭). On the phone, 他在吗？is the standard 'Is he there?'

content.name: precisely
content.side_f: I'm precisely Teacher Wang (speaking). (lit. I exactly am Wang teacher.)
content.side_g: 就 as an emphatic adverb confirms identity or narrows focus ('exactly/none other than'), very common in phone replies: 我就是 = 'Speaking.' Distinguish from 就 meaning 'then/right away' (他就来) — same character, different functions identified by position and context.

content.name: you_honorific
content.side_e: Nín hǎo, Lǐ xiānsheng zài ma?
content.side_g: 您 is the respectful form of 你, used for elders, customers, teachers, and strangers in formal settings. It has no plural 您们 in standard Mandarin — use 您几位 or 你们 instead. Overusing 您 with peers can sound distant or sarcastic.

content.name: which
content.side_e: Nín zhǎo nǎ wèi? / Něi ge lǎoshī?
content.side_f: Which one are you looking for? / Which teacher?
content.side_g: 哪 is the interrogative 'which', normally followed by a measure word + noun (哪本书, 哪位老师). Pronounced nǎ in careful speech; colloquially it fuses with 一 to become něi. Do not confuse with 那 (nà, 'that') — same radical, different tone and meaning.

content.name: polite_measure
content.side_e: Qǐng wèn nín shì nǎ wèi?
content.side_f: May I ask who is speaking? (lit. please ask you are which polite-MW?)
content.side_g: 位 is the polite measure word for people, implying respect (guests, teachers, customers). Never use 位 for oneself or for children/low-status referents — use 个 instead. Phone etiquette: 哪位? = 'Who's calling?'

content.name: afternoon
content.side_e: Jīntiān xiàwǔ nǐ yǒu shíjiān ma?
content.side_f: Do you have time this afternoon?
content.side_g: 下午 is a time-word (时间词), so it goes before the verb, not after: 我下午开会 ('I have a meeting in the afternoon'), never 我开会下午. No preposition 在 is required before time words. Contrasts with 上午 (morning) and 中午 (noon).

content.name: time
content.side_f: Sorry, I don't have time this afternoon.
content.side_g: 时间 refers to duration/availability of time, while 时候 (shíhou) refers to a moment/point in time (什么时候 = 'when'). 有时间 = 'have time (be free)'; negate with 没有时间, not 不有. Common collocations: 花时间 (spend time), 省时间 (save time).

content.name: question_problem
content.side_e: Wǒ yǒu yí ge wèntí xiǎng wèn nín.
content.side_f: I have a question to ask you.
content.side_g: 问题 covers both 'question' and 'problem/issue' — context disambiguates: 回答问题 (answer a question) vs. 有问题 (there's a problem). Measure word is 个. The noun 问 (wèn, ask) inside is the verb root; compare 提问题 (pose a question) and 没问题 ('no problem').

content.name: want_will
content.side_a: will, be going to; to want to, to have a desire to
content.side_f: I have a meeting tomorrow. / I'm going to have a meeting.
content.side_g: 要 has three overlapping senses: (1) future/intention 'be going to' (我要去); (2) desire 'want' (我要咖啡); (3) necessity 'must' (你要小心). Negate intention with 不 (不要 often means 'don't!'); negate past action with 没. Distinguish from 想 (xiǎng, would like) which is softer.

content.name: have_meeting
content.side_e: Wǒ xiàwǔ yào kāi huì, méiyǒu shíjiān.
content.side_f: I have a meeting this afternoon, so I have no time. (lit. open meeting)
content.side_g: 开会 is a separable verb-object (离合词): objects/duration go between 开 and 会 — 开了一个会, 开过几次会. Cannot take a direct object after 会; say 跟他开会 ('have a meeting with him'), not 开会他. Register is neutral — used in school and business.

content.name: morning
content.side_e: Wǒ shàngwǔ yǒu kè, xiàwǔ yǒu shíjiān.
content.side_f: I have class in the morning and am free in the afternoon.
content.side_g: 上午 refers to roughly 8am–noon; pair with 下午 (afternoon) and 晚上 (evening). As a time word, it precedes the verb without 在. Contrast with 早上 (zǎoshang, early morning, before work) — 上午 specifically denotes mid-morning working hours.

content.name: class_period
content.side_e: Wǒ shàngwǔ yǒu sān jié kè.
content.side_f: I have three class periods in the morning.
content.side_g: 节 is the measure word for class periods (一节课) and also segments of things in sections (一节车厢 'train car', 一节电池 'battery'). Do not use 个 for class periods. A 节课 is typically 45–50 minutes at Chinese schools.
